# 

# AI-SDD Process

## Spec Driven Development | Software Development Lifecycle with AI

[8.1 Resources Needed	16](#heading=)

1. # Overview {#overview}

The goal of this project is to introduce AI into the software development lifecycle through a controlled Spec Driven Development model. Rather than allowing AI agents to make ad hoc code changes, the organization will first define machine-readable specifications for systems, APIs, SNMP maps, UDP messages, protobuf contracts, Git flow, testing, and merge policies.

Once the specification foundation is in place, AI will be used to analyze historical tickets, Git activity, and the current codebase to identify recurring change patterns, architectural drift, test gaps, and opportunities for reuse. AI agents will then operate inside a standalone build environment where they can modify code, run tests, validate contracts, and produce evidence before changes are reviewed by humans.

The rollout culminates in an AI-enabled validation pipeline that includes unit tests, contract tests, SIL virtual environment testing, and controlled progression to real hardware testing. This creates a safer path for AI-assisted development while improving traceability, test coverage, documentation quality, and engineering throughput.

2. # Specification Foundation {#specification-foundation}

The first phase is to establish a specification-first operating model for all major software and system components. Without a formal API Development Specification, an AI agent can make changes that look technically correct but are operationally dangerous. This is accomplished by creating markdown files which contain detailed specifications for each sub-system, group of systems, interactions between systems, and intended behaviors. We will also create markdown files that define rules of engagement for agents to edit, comment, review, and propose changes to existing code. 

1. ## Define Specification for all Systems {#define-specification-for-all-systems}

Create a canonical specification structure that applies across software, firmware, APIs, protocols, test environments, and documentation.

Core specification types should include:

| Area | Specification Purpose |
| :---- | :---- |
| System Specification | Defines system behavior, boundaries, interfaces, constraints, and operating assumptions |
| Subsystem Specification | Breaks the system into smaller domains with clear responsibilities |
| Code Development Specification | Defines coding standards, architecture rules, reuse principles, and implementation expectations |
| Git Flow Specification | Defines branching, merge policy, review gates, release tagging, and rollback rules |
| API Specification | Defines REST/OpenAPI behavior and compatibility requirements |
| SNMP Specification | Defines SNMP map creation, editing, naming, validation, and versioning |
| UDP Message Specification | Defines JSON-based UDP message contracts and validation rules |
| Protocol Buffer Specification | Defines protobuf schema ownership, versioning, compatibility, and synchronization rules |
| Test Specification | Defines required unit, integration, SIL, HIL, and regression tests |
| AI Agent Specification | Defines what AI agents may change, how they validate changes, and what evidence they must produce |

3. # Existing Artifact Extraction {#existing-artifact-extraction}

Before AI agents can safely assist development, existing system knowledge must be extracted from current engineering artifacts. Sources include GIT repos, JIRA tickets, Confluence Pages. The idea is to first establish the designer’s intent so that an LLM can intuit certain design directions with human-in-the-loop decision making. Distilling intent makes the AI more useful when writing prompts for PRs, ticket creation and eventually coding. AS of this writing, LLMs can ground themselves with RAG servers and MD files. Most of the content will be distilled into MD files which will live with the code going forward.

1. ## Extract Existing API Definitions {#extract-existing-api-definitions}

Extract existing OpenAPI / Swagger YAML files and convert them into a formal API Development Specification.

| Item | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| API Purpose | What is the purpose of each API Endpoint  |
| API Request & Response Body | What are the shapes of each. Is there a standard or are there allowable exceptions |
| Endpoint behavior | Expected request, response, errors, and side effects |
| Compatibility policy | Rules for breaking vs non-breaking changes. Important for UI compatibility |
| Validation rules | Required linting and schema validation. Typically, we will reference OpenAPI Swagger Spec 3.0.3. But it needs to be explicitly defined |
| Test requirements | Required contract tests and regression tests. Best achieved with python test scripts embedded into the repo. Affected API endpoints must be tested after each change.  |

2. ## Extract Existing SNMP Map Files {#extract-existing-snmp-map-files}

Extract current SNMP maps and define a formal SNMP Creation and Editing Specification.

| Item | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| OID naming rules | How new identifiers are assigned |
| Field definitions | Type, range, units, and description requirements |
| Versioning policy | How SNMP changes are tracked |
| Validation rules | AI and human checks before merge |
| Regression testing | Required checks against existing consumers |

3. ## Extract UDP Message Contracts {#extract-udp-message-contracts}

Extract existing UDP message contracts into JSON form and define a UDP Message Contract Specification.

| Item | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Message schema | JSON structure, required fields, optional fields |
| Timing assumptions | Frequency, timeout, retry, packet-loss expectations |
| Compatibility rules | Additive vs breaking message changes |
| Error handling | Invalid, missing, malformed, or delayed messages |
| Test cases | Simulated packet scenarios |

4. ## Extract Protocol Buffer Messages {#extract-protocol-buffer-messages}

Extract existing protobuf messages and define a Protocol Buffer / ICP Specification for cross-application synchronization.

5. ## Extract Application Configuration  {#extract-application-configuration}

Each application has configuration parameters that influence runtime behavior, deployment behavior, environment-specific behavior, security posture, integration behavior, feature flags, logging, performance limits, hardware communication, and external system connectivity.

This is important because configuration often controls behavior that is not obvious from the code. An AI agent may correctly modify source code but accidentally break the system if it does not understand the required configuration, environment assumptions, or runtime constraints.

| Item | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Configuration inventory | List all environment variables, config files, command-line parameters, secrets, feature flags, and runtime settings |
| Ownership | Identify which team or role owns each configuration group |
| Default values | Document expected defaults and whether defaults are safe for development, test, staging, or production |
| Environment mapping | Define how configuration differs across local, CI, SIL, staging, hardware test, and production environments |
| Required vs optional settings | Identify which settings are mandatory for startup and which are optional |
| Validation rules | Define type, range, format, and allowed values for each configuration parameter |
| Secret handling | Identify secrets, credentials, tokens, certificates, and keys that must not be exposed to AI agents or logs |
| Runtime impact | Explain what each parameter affects, including performance, connectivity, hardware behavior, safety, logging, and retry behavior |
| Change control | Define which configuration changes require peer review, security review, or hardware validation |
| Test requirements | Define tests required when configuration defaults or validation rules change |

6. ## Analyze Database Schema Design  {#analyze-database-schema-design}

Analyze the existing database schema and convert findings into a formal Database Schema and Data Model Specification. AI-generated code changes can break data integrity, query performance, reporting, synchronization, and rollback if the database model is not explicitly understood. This specification is essential for defining the boundaries of schema changes. 

| Item | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Schema inventory | List tables, views, indexes, constraints, stored procedures, triggers, migrations, and data ownership boundaries |
| Data model purpose | Explain what business or system concepts each table represents |
| Entity relationships | Document primary keys, foreign keys, cardinality, and ownership relationships |
| Naming standards | Define naming rules for tables, columns, indexes, constraints, and migration files |
| Data types | Review whether column types are appropriate for expected values, precision, scale, and storage requirements |
| Constraints | Document required, nullable, unique, check, and referential integrity rules |
| Indexing strategy | Analyze whether indexes support expected query patterns and performance requirements |
| Migration policy | Define how schema changes are created, reviewed, tested, versioned, and rolled back |
| Compatibility rules | Define safe vs breaking database changes |
| Performance risks | Identify large tables, slow queries, missing indexes, redundant indexes, and high-risk joins |
| Data retention | Document retention, archival, deletion, and soft-delete policies |
| Security and compliance | Identify sensitive data, access control rules, encryption requirements, and audit requirements |
| Test requirements | Define migration tests, rollback tests, seed data tests, and query regression tests |

7. ## Analyze Documentation  {#analyze-documentation}

Analyze existing documentation and convert it into a formal Documentation Quality and Coverage Specification. This is important because AI agents are only as reliable as the context they are given. If documentation is stale, incomplete, or contradictory, the AI may follow the wrong instructions and produce unsafe changes. This stage brings to light inconsistencies in the user experience. Upon adding new features, we want to ensure that the documentation evolves in lock step with the application changes. Not all application changes have user impact. So this analysis ensures that any public interfaces or client SLAs are maintained as firm contracts which will create a natural guard rail for future changes which can be caught during PR, QA, IVV or FSAT.

| Item | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Documentation inventory | List README files, architecture docs, deployment guides, API docs, runbooks, troubleshooting guides, onboarding docs, and user-facing documentation |
| Documentation ownership | Identify who owns each document and how it is maintained |
| Accuracy review | Compare documentation against current code, configuration, APIs, database schemas, and deployment behavior |
| Coverage gaps | Identify missing documentation for critical workflows, interfaces, environments, and operational procedures |
| Outdated content | Flag stale, contradictory, duplicated, or misleading documentation |
| AI usability | Determine whether documentation is structured well enough for AI agents to follow safely |
| Human usability | Determine whether engineers can use the documentation to build, test, deploy, troubleshoot, and rollback |
| Required templates | Define standard templates for specs, runbooks, tickets, pull requests, test evidence, and release notes |
| Update rules | Define when documentation must be updated as part of a code, API, schema, config, or deployment change |
| Review requirements | Define documentation review gates before merge or release |

4. # Historical Analysis {#historical-analysis}

A validation period to ensure that change history is understood by the agents. Understanding the current state of the application does not paint the full picture for a coding agent. Experimentation shows that giving Agent access to historic record of changes, informs decision making for future changes. It also allows the Agent to highlight new changes that can introduce bugs, re-introduce previously solved issues, etc. This step will allow AI to analyze the relationship between engineering intent, ticket history, and actual code changes.

Implementation Details: Migrate to github from bitbucket to leverage semantic search capability. 

1. ## Analyze Ticket History {#analyze-ticket-history}

Analyze historical tickets to identify:

| Analysis Area | Goal |
| :---- | :---- |
| Recurring change types | Understand common features, bugs, and maintenance patterns |
| Ambiguous requirements | Identify where tickets lacked enough detail |
| Rework causes | Find where poor specifications caused repeated changes |
| Testing gaps | Identify where tickets lacked validation criteria |
| Domain vocabulary | Extract common engineering language and system concepts |

2. ## Analyze GIT History {#analyze-git-history}

Analyze recent Git history to understand current development direction.

| Analysis Area | Goal |
| :---- | :---- |
| High-change modules | Identify unstable or actively evolving areas |
| Merge patterns | Understand how changes currently flow |
| Defect-prone areas | Identify files with frequent fixes |
| Architectural drift | Find where implementation diverges from design intent |
| Test coverage relationship | Compare changed code against test additions |

3. ## Analyze Current Code State {#analyze-current-code-state}

Perform AI-assisted codebase analysis for:

| Analysis Area | Goal |
| :---- | :---- |
| Performance | Identify inefficient logic, bottlenecks, and risky runtime behavior |
| Redundancy | Find duplicate logic and opportunities for reuse |
| Architecture | Compare code against expected subsystem boundaries |
| Maintainability | Identify overly complex or fragile areas |
| Testability | Identify code that is difficult to validate or simulate |
| Compliance with principles | Check naming, structure, patterns, and development rules |

5. # AI Agent Development Environment {#ai-agent-development-environment}

The goal of this section is to create a controlled environment where AI agents can safely modify, build, and test code before human review. Where it’s new code, or existing code, a development environment where the agent can test changes is critical. Unit Test, Functional Test and system test become more important when relying on agents to make QA or coding decisions. They must be able to see the impact of their changes, via logs, screenshots etc to be able to make adequate changes. The build environment ensures agents can see the results of their decision making at compile time.

1. ## Create Stand Alone Build Environment {#create-stand-alone-build-environment}

Build a deterministic AI-agent-compatible development environment.

| Requirement | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Isolated workspace | AI agents work in a disposable environment |
| Reproducible build | Same dependencies, compiler versions, tools, and scripts |
| No uncontrolled production access | AI cannot directly modify production systems |
| Local validation | Agents can build, test, lint, and generate reports |
| Artifact capture | Build logs, test logs, diffs, and validation output are retained |

2. ## Define AI Agent Operating Rules {#define-ai-agent-operating-rules}

The AI agent environment should enforce rules such as:

| Requirement | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Specification-first | AI must read the relevant specification before making changes |
| Ticket-bound work | AI only works against approved tickets |
| Diff discipline | AI must produce understandable, reviewable changes |
| Test evidence | AI must run required tests and report results |
| No silent assumptions | AI must document assumptions and uncertainties |
| Human approval gate | AI cannot merge directly without approval |

3. ## Unit Test Framework for AI Agents {#unit-test-framework-for-ai-agents}

Create or extend unit test frameworks so AI agents can validate their work automatically.

| Requirement | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Unit tests | Validate individual functions and classes |
| Contract tests | Validate APIs, protobufs, UDP messages, and SNMP behavior |
| Regression tests | Ensure existing behavior is not broken |
| Static analysis | Check coding standards, security risks, and maintainability |
| Build validation | Confirm the project compiles cleanly |
| Change-specific tests | Tests directly tied to the ticket acceptance criteria |

4. ## AI Generated Test Requirements {#ai-generated-test-requirements}

Each ticket should define:

| Requirement | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Acceptance criteria | What must be true for the ticket to be complete |
| Required tests | Unit, integration, contract, or SIL tests required |
| Risk areas | Modules or behaviors likely to be affected |
| Validation evidence | Logs, screenshots, test reports, or simulation output required |

## 

5. ## Agent Skills {#agent-skills}

Each ticket should define:

| Requirement | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Coding Design Pattern |  |
| SW QA | Define and run tests alongside implementation |
| Pull Request | Include context, justification, impact analysis, spec IDs |
| Coding Style Guide | Compliance with project-defined linting, naming, formatting rules |
| Coding Style Guide Validation | Run tools (ruff, checkstyle) and settings before submitting code for review |

6. ## MCP Servers {#mcp-servers}

Each ticket should define:

| Requirement | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Context7 MCP | Latest Documentation for code documentation |
| Atlassian MCP Server | Jira Ticket History |
| Github MCP | Read repositories |
| Postgres | Ability to Communicate in SQL language |

6. # QA & Testing {#qa-&-testing}

When introducing AI Agent’s generated changes to real hardware, it’s important to ensure that there are gates preventing hardware abuse. At this stage a human-in-the-loop is necessary. Hardware can be bricked requiring XJTAG intervention. If a brick event were to occur over the weekend due to an unattended agent deployment, the lost time may result in a late customer delivery. Therefore, QA testing must introduce more controls over agent outputs.

1. ## Utilize SIL Virtual Environment {#utilize-sil-virtual-environment}

Use the SIL environment to validate AI-generated changes, QA dispositions for physical hardware testing.

| Requirement | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Virtualized system behavior | Simulate hardware-adjacent behavior |
| Message replay | Replay UDP, protobuf, SNMP, or API traffic |
| Fault injection | Simulate packet loss, invalid values, timeouts, and degraded states |
| Regression scenarios | Validate known system behaviors |
| Hardware-readiness gate | Determine whether code is safe to test on real hardware |

2. ## Real Hardware Validation {#real-hardware-validation}

After SIL validation, define a controlled path to real hardware testing.

| Requirement | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Unit tests pass | Basic correctness verified |
| Contract tests pass | Interface compatibility verified |
| SIL tests pass | Behavior validated in simulated environment |
| Human review complete | Engineer approves logic and risk |
| Hardware test authorized | Change can be tested on real hardware |
| Reboot Testing | Restarting running hardware during normal operation in order to monitor startup sequence or to purposefully introduce run-time failure. |

7. # AI Ticket Creation Workflow {#ai-ticket-creation-workflow}

Software development follows a controlled set of standard procedures for introducing, reviewing, validating, delivering, and auditing changes. This is the practical definition of the Software Development Life Cycle. A change should be traceable from the original task or ticket all the way through implementation, validation, delivery, and release.

In an AI-enabled Spec Driven Development model, the ticket becomes more important, not less important. The ticket is the entry point for change. It defines the desired outcome, acceptance criteria, affected systems, expected tests, and evidence required before the work can be considered complete.

However, the ticket does not stand alone. Under the hood, every ticket-driven change must pass through a controlled software delivery workflow that includes branch creation, versioning, pull request review, rebasing, merge control, validation builds, artifact delivery, and audit logging.

This becomes especially important when AI agents participate in the SDLC. The AI agent’s responsibility is not only to help describe the ticket or generate implementation work. The agent must also help define verification criteria, identify affected specifications, produce test evidence, and document how the completed change satisfies the original intent.

Previously, much of this responsibility was handled implicitly by a senior software engineer or software engineering manager. In an AI-assisted workflow, that judgment must be converted into explicit rules, checklists, templates, and validation gates.

1. ## Ticket Formatting {#ticket-formatting}

Every software change should begin with a ticket. The ticket defines the business, operational, technical, or defect-driven reason for the change. A ticket should not simply describe a task. It should define the intended outcome. The ticket becomes the traceability anchor for the entire change lifecycle.

The AI should help convert loose requests into structured engineering work. For example, it can take a field issue, engineering notes, customer requests, test failures, or code analysis results and transform it into a properly structured ticket. An AI Agent may draft a ticket, but the organization must approve the ticket before the AI Agent or the engineer begins implementing it.

| Requirement | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Change objective | What needs to change and why |
| Affected system | Repo, service, subsystem, API, protocol, database, or configuration area impacted |
| Specification references | Links to the relevant system, API, protocol, configuration, database, or testing specification |
| Acceptance criteria | Conditions that must be true for the ticket to be considered complete |
| Verification criteria | Tests, build steps, reviews, simulations, or artifacts required to prove the work |
| Risk classification | Low, medium, high, or hardware-impacting |
| Compatibility impact | Whether the change affects APIs, protocols, schemas, configs, or downstream consumers |
| Rollback expectation | How the change can be reverted or mitigated |
| Evidence requirements | Logs, test reports, screenshots, build artifacts, SIL reports, or review notes |
| Open Questions | Assumptions or missing details that require human clarification |

1. ### Quality {#quality}

A weak ticket creates weak AI output. If the ticket is vague, incomplete, or missing verification criteria, the AI may produce code that appears correct but fails to satisfy the real objective.

Generally this can be solved by asking an agent the question: “How can you improve this ticket”. Using multiple models to validate the quality of a ticket is also important. It is a form of thought diversity. Just like in human-run organizations, diversity of thought yields better conclusions.

| Requirement | Addresses |
| :---- | :---- |
| Clear objective | AI may solve the wrong problem |
| Acceptance criteria | Review becomes subjective |
| Verification criteria | Completion cannot be proven |
| Affected system | AI may inspect or modify the wrong area |
| Specification reference | AI lacks the correct operating constraints |
| Risk level | Unsafe changes may bypass proper review |
| Test expectations | AI may skip required validation |
| Rollback expectation | Failed changes may be difficult to recover from |

2. ## Minimum Requirements for Making a Good Ticket {#minimum-requirements-for-making-a-good-ticket}

Create a guide that explains how engineers, product owners, and managers should ask AI to create tickets.

| Requirement | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| Good ticket format | Required fields and structure |
| Bad ticket examples | Examples of vague or unsafe requests |
| Acceptance criteria | How to describe done conditions |
| Test expectations | How to specify validation requirements |
| Risk classification | How to flag risky changes |
| Specification links | How to connect tickets to relevant specifications |

3. ## Governance {#governance}

The purpose of an AI Driven SDLC is to delegate all coding assignments to the AI Coding agent with human observability via Pull requests, metric dashboard, feature reports, release report and other documentation. This process is akin to a factory where the operators have clear input/output expectations and can control the velocity of work by adjusting system parameters.

When we transform the developer's role from a *builder* to an *architect and inspector* we need to build in tooling for governance that validates the architecture design is in-line with the SDD architecture docs. The SDD must require strict adherence to full code review with explanations for why the code is either rejected or passes review. We need to have dashboards in Grafana generated at each step as part of the governance process which clearly show the actions of the agents. 

Agents must have a retry process to fix issues before moving to HITL (human in the loop) in which if a ticket’s changes are rejected more than a set number of times then the agent flags the ticket and PR for human review.

4. ## Benchmarking

[https://bitbucket.eng.thinkom.com/users/mchung/repos/ai-benchmark/browse](https://bitbucket.eng.thinkom.com/users/mchung/repos/ai-benchmark/browse)

5. ## SLDC Observability {#sldc-observability}

SDD observability is critical because when AI writes the code, code is no longer the source of truth—the specification is. Therefore, full observability in SDD must shift from observing software execution to observing the translation of intent into execution. 

Observability in this context means that we need to be able to trace a block of the spec to token usage and specific lines of code in a PR –AND/OR– be able to trace lines of code in a PR back to an agent ID, a prompt template and the section of the specification.

This also requires divergence/orphan scoring–i.e. what cannot be mapped clearly back to the specification. The SDD will require a specification that defines limits for divergence and generates a report as part of each PR.

**Forecasting AI Unit Economics**. 

Executive leadership establishes clear visibility into the *"Cost per Feature,"* facilitating accurate projections for engineering expansion. Management can identify specific prompt iterations or model deployments that negatively impact the First-Pass Success Rate or result in inefficient, costly self-correction cycles.

**AST-Based Tagging & Metadata Injection**

Implement a linter or compiler extension designed to embed distinct Requirement IDs (*req\_id*) directly into the Abstract Syntax Tree (AST). These identifiers should be injected as inline documentation tags or metadata (e.g., *// @spec\[REQ-102\]*), ensuring every line of synthesized code is traceable to the underlying specification. Requirement IDs will be taken from the heading in the spec (i.e. “AI-SSD-7.4”)

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abstract\_syntax\_tree](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abstract_syntax_tree)

**Agent Clarity and Currency decision gate**

An explicit gate the agent must evaluate before it treats a spec as having enough authority to proceed. An agent may continue only when all four of the follow are true.

**Proceeds only when the agent can say:**

"For this task, spec \<filename\>@\<version\> section \<section\> requires \<behavior\>,and the implementation will satisfy it by \<specific action/test\>."

**File feedback when the agent has to say:**

"I need to assume/choose/guess whether \<decision\> should be \<option A\> or \<option B\>."

| Condition | The agent asks | Proceed when | File feedback when |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Clear | "Can I identify the required behavior without choosing between multiple reasonable interpretations?" | The relevant section gives a single actionable rule, requirement, or acceptance criterion. | The spec uses vague language, leaves a key decision open, or could reasonably support more than one implementation. |
| Complete | "Do I have enough information to implement and test this task?" | Inputs, outputs, boundaries, non-goals, failure behavior, and acceptance expectations are sufficient for the change. | The spec names the feature but omits necessary details such as status codes, data shape, ownership, security behavior, or test expectations. |
| Consistent | "Do all governing specs that apply to this task agree?" | Global, project-type, and project-scoped specs can all be followed at the same time. | Two specs, two sections, or a spec and a project override require incompatible behavior. |
| Current | "Does the spec still match the codebase, platform, dependencies, APIs, and project reality I can observe?" | Referenced files, APIs, commands, architecture, and operational assumptions still exist or are intentionally targeted future state. | The spec points to removed APIs, renamed files, obsolete workflows, old provider/model names, stale infrastructure, or behavior the registry has superseded. |

**Telemetry tied to model usage, duration and agent ID:**

| Metric | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| TTFT & Latency | Time to First Token and total generation duration per agent step. |
| Token Efficiency Ratio | Ratio of prompt tokens to generated code tokens (detecting over-verbose prompting). |
| Cost per Feature/Fix | Total financial cost (in USD) of LLM API calls required to successfully merge a specific specification. |
| Tokens miss | Tokens wasted on blind alley issues, rewrites and bad output. |
| Self-Healing Iteration Rate | The number of loops an agent takes (Compilation → Failure →  Prompt Correction →  Re-generation) before hitting a passing state. |
| First-Pass Success Rate (FPSR) | Percentage of specs that successfully compile and pass all generated tests on iteration 0\. |
| Iterations (PSR) | Number of iterations to successfully compile and pass all generated tests. |
| Self-Healing Convergence Rate | A metric tracking whether subsequent iterations are reducing compiler/test error counts, or if the agent is stuck in an infinite hallucinatory loop. |
| Spec Compliance | A quantitative score (0-100%) assigned by an independent evaluation layer assessing how thoroughly the generated code covers the constraints defined in the spec.  *Note: How do we measure this qualitative metric* |
| Semantic drift | Compute the vector distance between the structural intent of the new code and the established architecture. Semantic Drift Pipeline: Compute the vector distance between the structural intent of new code and the established architecture. Includes AST/LLM architectural summarization of codebase changes in CI, vector embedding generation (OpenAI compatible /v1/embeddings or local hash), and cosine distance comparison against active spec vectors to output a telemetry drift score (0.0 to 1.0, 0.0 being no drift) pushed to the /metrics endpoint. *Note: How do we measure this qualitative metric* |
| Architecture boundary violations | Number of times an agent has violated the spec via LLM analysis of git diff to .x\`\` Note: How do we measure this or flag this. And what do we do? Do we reject the PR. |
| Spec error attribution | Errors or regressions traced back to vague or contradictory specification nodes.  Phase A: Spec Development Phase B: Spec Review Phase C: Coding Phase D: QAx\`x\` |
| Root cause singularity | Classification metric for a failure from Logic gap where the code didn’t match the spec or context gap where the code did match the spec but the spec didn’t account for the real-world state. |
| Spec flaw | Signal for errors in code that has a perfect spec compliance score. Identify problems with the spec. |

**Detailed Diagrams**

**[Joel De Gan](mailto:joel.degan@thinkom.com)add diagrams here by eod 6/18/2026**

![][image1]

8. # Schedule {#schedule}

16 Week Phased Approach for AI Integrated into SDLC

* Phase 1: Specification Baseline \[4 week\]  
* Phase 2: Artifact Extraction \[2 weeks\]  
* Phase 3: Historical Content Analysis \[2 weeks\]  
* Phase 4: AI Agent Build/Test Environment \[2 weeks\]  
* Phase 5: Ticket Creation & Governance \[2 week\]  
* Phase 6: SIL & Hardware Validation \[8 weeks\]

![][image2]

![][image3]

1. ## Resources Needed

* 1 Software Engineer (Chimdi for now, or introduce others?)  
* Dev Ops Engineering (Rich, Joel, Mitch)  
* Product Hardware (Depending on Application) \- Antenna, BFD, etc.  
* Virtual Machine Environment (existing)

**Summary**

| [Specification Foundation](#bookmark=id.apgbpurqfj5) |
| :---- |
| [Define Specification for all Systems](#bookmark=id.cgbtfnjf2pab) |

9. # Appendix Tokenomics

The Linux Foundation Project launched Tokenomics Foundation in June 2026 which will bring FinOps-style disciplines applied to AI cost management.  

| Name | Resource |
| :---- | :---- |
| Tokenomics Foundation (Linux Foundation) | https://www.tokeneconomics.com/ |
| Conference: June 8-11 2026 in San DiegoAI Value: The Era of FinOps for AI, Token Economics, and Agentic FinOps | https://x.finops.org/ |
